long_description: Adapter Kind: Adapter Instance: Infrastructure Health Adapter - vRealize Operations Collector-vrops-master - Collecting - Data receiving VMWARE - w006-vc.w006.parpr.dir - Collecting - Data receiving vRealizeOpsMgrAPI (vRealize Operations Collector-vrops-master) - Collecting - Data receiving pSAN - w006-vc.w006.parpr.dir - Collecting - Data receiving Alert Description: The host lost connectivity to one or more NFS servers. The NFS volumes and the virtual machines on the affected volumes might be unavailable to the server. This fault typically occurs in an environment where NFS was previously working and NFS connectivity was lost. Alert ID: 3d261310-353b-446c-8ad8-6c714f3db22c Alert Impact: health Alert Name: The host lost connectivity to a Network File System (NFS) server - IBM Alert Subtype: Availability Alert Type: Network Cancel Date: Unavailable Control State: Open Criticality: critical Host Name: vrops-data01.parpr.dir Alert Link: https://172.22.2.52/vcf-operations/ui/inventory;mode=hierarchy;resourceId=ac060473-838c-4fe6-84c6-d31970d07f7e;tab=alerts-and-symptoms;subTab=alerts;entityId=3d261310-353b-446c-8ad8-6c714f3db22c Notification Rule Description: Notification Rule Name: PD: The host lost connectivity to a Network File System (NFS) server Object Efficiency State: 1.0 Object Health State: 4.0 Object Risk State: 2.0 Product: ['N/A', 'N/A', 'N/A'] Recommendations: Runbook: https://pages.github.ibm.com/cloudlab/internal-docs/vrops_sev1_alerts.html#the-host-lost-connectivity-to-a-network-file-system-nfs-server | Verify that the NFS server is running. | Check the networking configuration to verify that the ESX host can connect to the NFS server. | Determine whether the other hosts that use the same NFS mount are experiencing the same problem, then check the NFS server status and share points. | Make sure that you can reach the NFS server by logging into the service console and using vmkping to ping the NFS server: "vmkping [nfs server]". | For advanced troubleshooting information, see VMware KB 1003967. Runbook URL: https://pages.github.ibm.com/cloudlab/internal-docs/vrops_sev1_alerts.html#the-host-lost-connectivity-to-a-network-file-system-nfs-server Resource ID: ac060473-838c-4fe6-84c6-d31970d07f7e Resource Kind: HostSystem Resource Name: w006p06nsx000.w006.parpr.dir Status: Active Symptoms: Symptom Set - self Symptoms Lost connection to NFS server | w006p06nsx000.w006.parpr.dir | ac060473-838c-4fe6-84c6-d31970d07f7e : Lost connection to NFS server Recommendations: Runbook: https://pages.github.ibm.com/cloudlab/internal-docs/vrops_sev1_alerts.html#the-host-lost-connectivity-to-a-network-file-system-nfs-server | Verify that the NFS server is running. | Check the networking configuration to verify that the ESX host can connect to the NFS server. | Determine whether the other hosts that use the same NFS mount are experiencing the same problem, then check the NFS server status and share points. | Make sure that you can reach the NFS server by logging into the service console and using vmkping to ping the NFS server: "vmkping [nfs server]". | For advanced troubleshooting information, see VMware KB 1003967.
